questions.question: Why is 'high scale' by itself an incomplete requirement?
options: Because it does not reveal whether the system is read-heavy, write-heavy, bursty, fanout-heavy, or bandwidth-intensive.
Because only low-scale systems need architecture decisions.
Because interviews are not allowed to mention scale without exact hardware counts.
Because storage growth is always more important than traffic shape.
questions.explanation: Scale only becomes actionable when translated into a concrete workload pattern.

questions.question: Which workload characteristic is most likely to change a feed system architecture?
options: Whether the design uses rounded or square boxes.
Whether writes fan out to many followers or reads assemble personalized timelines on demand.
Whether the repository uses JSON or YAML quizzes.
Whether the lesson title mentions Google or Cloudflare first.
questions.explanation: Fanout strategy is central to feed workload shape and architecture.

questions.question: Why should the primary user journey be translated into backend operations before architecture?
options: Because it reveals request mix, amplification, and latency-sensitive paths that the design must support.
Because it replaces the need for any failure analysis.
Because it guarantees a single correct storage model.
Because it makes trade-offs less important.
questions.explanation: The journey becomes useful only when mapped to reads, writes, queues, and hotspots.

questions.question: What is the main risk of sizing only the average case?
options: The system may look correct on paper while bursts, hotspots, or fanout spikes still break it.
It makes the write path too durable.
It prevents any observability plan from being created.
It always overestimates hardware cost.
questions.explanation: Average-only thinking hides the operational stressors that often dominate design.

questions.question: Which metric best detects whether workload shape was captured well enough?
options: Whether the answer names read/write mix, amplification, burst factor, and likely hotspots before component details expand.
The number of load balancers in the diagram.
The number of glossary terms referenced.
The total number of markdown headings.
questions.explanation: Those fields convert vague scale into architecture-ready workload shape.

questions.question: Why is fanout factor dangerous to ignore?
options: Because one user action can generate far more backend work than its input rate suggests.
Because fanout only matters for object storage products.
Because it eliminates the need for queues.
Because it makes burst factor irrelevant.
questions.explanation: Amplification can dominate throughput, storage, and queue sizing.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with explicitly separating synchronous and asynchronous workload paths?
options: It improves latency reasoning and failure analysis, but adds a bit more complexity to the explanation.
It makes the data model unnecessary.
It guarantees eventual consistency even when not desired.
It only helps systems with no user-facing reads.
questions.explanation: The extra structure is worth it because it clarifies what must be fast now versus processed later.

questions.question: If one tenant suddenly becomes 30% of total traffic, what workload concern becomes more important?
options: Hotspot and skew handling, because average fleet load no longer describes local stress accurately.
Markdown formatting consistency.
Whether the quiz has enough pre questions.
Whether the system still has a single core workflow.
questions.explanation: Skew changes the effective stress profile even when overall QPS looks manageable.
